hypotheses.discriminating_prediction: Non-invasive modulation of brain pulsatility (e.g., low-frequency ultrasound, specific respiratory exercises) in aged animals will enhance perivascular ISF flow and improve the spatial distribution homogeneity of fluorescently-labeled NTFs within specific cortical layers, leading to enhanced LTP induction in previously 'NTF-deficient' regions.
hypotheses.feasibility_note: Glymphatic flow can be imaged in vivo (e.g., using MRI or two-photon microscopy with tracers). Low-frequency ultrasound and respiratory modulation are existing techniques. This requires precise measurement of local NTF distribution.
hypotheses.notes: Proposes that distribution, not just delivery, is the problem due to fluid dynamics. Discriminating prediction: NTF delivery might be fine at the BBB, but its spread is impaired. Challenges the assumption that 'delivery' equals 'bioavailability at the synapse'.

hypotheses.process_hypothesis: SCOUT - In the aging neurovascular unit, aberrant mechanosensing by pericytes, triggered by changes in vessel stiffness and blood flow shear stress, leads to a maladaptive 'mechano-transcriptional' switch. This switch downregulates the expression of specific glutamate transporter subtypes (e.g., GLAST/EAAT1 on astrocytes) and neurotrophic factor receptors on adjacent astrocytes and neurons (SPV_1, SPV_2), effectively 'blinding' them to available factors, even if their systemic delivery and local concentration are adequate.
hypotheses.discriminating_prediction: In vitro co-culture models mimicking aged vessel stiffness and shear stress will show decreased GLAST/EAAT1 expression and TrkB receptor levels in astrocytes/neurons cultured alongside pericytes, preventable by inhibiting specific mechanotransduction pathways (e.g., YAP/TAZ, Piezo channels) in pericytes, even when NTFs are supplied exogenously. This effect would not be seen with normal stiffness/shear stress.
hypotheses.feasibility_note: In vitro co-culture models allow manipulation of mechanical cues. Gene expression analysis (qPCR, Western blot) and receptor quantification are standard. Specific mechanotransduction inhibitors are available for in vitro use.
hypotheses.notes: Proposes that the NVM doesn't just block or transport, but actively 'informs' nearby glia/neurons about its aged state, leading to a reduced response capacity. Challenges the idea that simply increasing NTF/glutamate levels will suffice; the 'sensing' machinery itself is compromised.

questions.text: Does selective pharmacogenetic activation of pericyte-endothelial cell adhesion proteins (e.g., N-cadherin) in aged mouse models restore the selective permeability of the BBB to systemically administered 10-20 kDa fluorescently-tagged neurotrophic factors (NTFs) while maintaining exclusion of 10 kDa dextrans, and does this correlate with improved synaptic long-term potentiation (LTP) in the hippocampus?
questions.rationale: This question directly tests IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_01's premise that pericyte detachment and structural changes are primary drivers of selective NTF impediment. If restored adhesion improves selective permeability and LTP, it supports IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_01 over energetic (IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_02) or systemic (IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_03) issues as the sole limiting factor. If permeability is restored but LTP isn't, it implies other factors are at play.
questions.feasibility_note: Pharmacogenetic tools for cell-specific manipulation, fluorescent tracers of different sizes, BBB permeability assays, and in vivo electrophysiology in aged rodents are all established techniques. N-cadherin modulation is a known target.

questions.text: Can targeted delivery of a cell-permeable ATP precursor (e.g., specialized creatine analog or NAD+ booster) specifically to brain endothelial cells in aged organ-on-chip models of the NVU increase the trans-endothelial transport rate of NTFs and the clearance rate of glutamate, independent of tight junction integrity, when compared to controls or pan-cellular delivery?
questions.rationale: This question directly probes IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_02. If endothelial-specific energetic boosting enhances transport without direct structural changes or mechanosensing modulation, it supports an energetic bottleneck. Organ-on-chip models allow precise control over specific cell types and targeted delivery, distinguishing it from general BBB structural issues (IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_01) or receptor sensitivity (IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_05).
questions.feasibility_note: NVU organ-on-chip models are available. Endothelial-targeted delivery is achievable with nanoparticles. ATP levels, transport kinetics, and glutamate clearance can be measured in these systems.

questions.text: Does oral supplementation with specific SCFA analogs (e.g., butyrate) in aged mice with gut dysbiosis simultaneously reduce peripheral inflammatory markers, restore BBB integrity (measured by albumin leakage), increase brain capillary TrkB receptor expression on pericytes and astrocytes, and enhance brain uptake of systemically administered NTFs, ultimately leading to improved spatial memory, compared to placebo or direct brain NTF infusion?
questions.rationale: This tests the multi-faceted systemic hypothesis (IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_03). If SCFA supplementation elicits a broad range of beneficial effects including BBB integrity, receptor expression, NTF uptake, and cognitive improvement, it strongly supports the gut-brain axis as a primary modulator, distinguishing it from purely structural (IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_01) or distribution (IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_04) issues.
questions.feasibility_note: Aged mouse models, oral supplementation, peripheral and brain biomarker analysis, BBB leakage assays (e.g., Evans Blue, albumin), TrkB immunohistochemistry, and behavioral assays for memory are all standard.

questions.text: If fluorescently-tagged NTFs are directly injected into the brain parenchyma of aged mice, does targeted, non-invasive enhancement of microvascular pulsatility (e.g., focused ultrasound, modulated respiration) improve the spatial homogeneity and effective diffusion distance of these NTFs within specific cortical layers, leading to enhanced region-specific LTP induction in previously 'NTF-deficient' areas, compared to non-pulsatility-enhanced controls?
questions.rationale: This directly tests IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_04 by bypassing BBB delivery altogether. If local distribution and effective reach of NTFs are improved by modulating ISF flow dynamics (microvascular pulsatility), and this translates to functional synaptic recovery, it indicates that impaired distribution is a key bottleneck, distinct from (though potentially additive to) BBB integrity (IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_01) or active transport (IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_02) issues.
questions.feasibility_note: Intraparenchymal injections are standard. Glymphatic flow imaging (e.g., 2-photon microscopy with fluorescent tracers), focused ultrasound, and in vivo LTP measurements are feasible in rodents.

questions.text: In a 3D co-culture model of the NVU with astrocytes and neurons, does mimicking aged vessel stiffness and shear stress via hydrogel modifications induce a decrease in astrocyte GLAST/EAAT1 expression and neuronal TrkB receptor levels, and can this be reversed by pharmacological inhibition of pericyte Piezo1/2 channels, even when NTFs are continuously supplied at supra-physiological concentrations, thereby improving glutamate uptake and synaptic strength?
questions.rationale: This question directly tests IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_05's core idea of maladaptive mechanosensing. By providing excess NTFs and controlling for transport, the experiment isolates the 'sensing' mechanism. If inhibiting pericyte mechanotransduction restores receptor and transporter expression and function despite aged mechanical cues, it supports a primary role for aberrant mechanosensing, distinguishing it from transport or barrier issues.
questions.feasibility_note: 3D NVU co-culture systems are advanced but available. Hydrogel stiffness and shear stress manipulation are standard. Gene/protein expression analysis, functional glutamate uptake assays, and synaptic activity measurements are feasible. Piezo channel inhibitors are established research tools.

questions.text: Does aging-associated systemic microRNA dysregulation, transported via extracellular vesicles from peripheral immune cells, directly reprogram specific endothelial or pericyte gene expression in the neurovascular unit, leading to a breakdown of both selective permeability and active transport mechanisms (NTF delivery and glutamate clearance) that is not fully explained by structural, energetic, or mechanosensing changes alone?
questions.rationale: This question explores a 'hidden medium' – microRNAs and extracellular vesicles – that could integrate and exacerbate multiple failure modes across the BBB, potentially acting as a systemic regulator bridging different IHs. It challenges the existing IHs by proposing a novel, upstream regulatory mechanism for NVU dysfunction.
questions.feasibility_note: Isolation and analysis of extracellular vesicles from aged vs. young systemic circulation, co-culture experiments with NVU cells to assess miRNA transfer and target gene expression, and functional assays for BBB integrity and transport are feasible. Manipulating specific miRNAs in vitro is also possible.

l5_nodes.text: Does pharmacogenetic activation of N-cadherin in pericytes of aged mice selectively enhance BBB permeability to 10-20 kDa neurotrophic factors (NTFs) without compromising the exclusion of 10 kDa inert dextrans, and what are the cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying this selective permeability modulation?

Does pharmacogenetic activation of pericyte N-cadherin, leading to enhanced selective BBB permeability for neurotrophic factors, translate into improved hippocampal synaptic long-term potentiation (LTP) and cognitive function in aged mice, and what are the underlying cellular and molecular changes in synaptic plasticity markers?

Are there off-target effects of pericyte-specific N-cadherin activation on other cerebrovascular components (e.g., endothelial cells, astrocytes) or systemic physiology that could confound the observed BBB permeability and synaptic plasticity outcomes?

l6_tasks.title: Pharmacogenetic N-cadherin Activation and Selective BBB Permeability in Aged Mice

simt_parameters.system: Aged C57BL/6J mice (Jackson Labs, 18-20 months old, n=10 per group) expressing Cre recombinase under a pericyte-specific promoter (e.g., Pdgfrb-CreERT2) crossed with a floxed DREADD (Designer Receptors Exclusively Activated by Designer Drugs) construct for inducible N-cadherin overexpression (e.g., AAV-DIO-hM3Dq-N-cadherin). Mice will be housed under standard conditions with a 12h light/dark cycle. Age-matched wild-type mice will serve as controls.
simt_parameters.intervention: Pericyte-specific N-cadherin overexpression will be induced via tamoxifen administration (75 mg/kg, i.p., daily for 5 days) in Pdgfrb-CreERT2;hM3Dq-N-cadherin mice. After 2 weeks washout, CNO (clozapine-N-oxide, 1 mg/kg, i.p., daily for 7 days) will be administered to activate the DREADD. Control groups will receive vehicle (saline) or CNO in Cre-negative littermates. On day 7, mice will receive intravenous injections of 10 kDa FITC-dextran (Sigma-Aldrich, 100 µL of 10 mg/mL) and 15 kDa Alexa Fluor 555-tagged BDNF (Alomone Labs, 100 µL of 1 mg/mL) via tail vein. Animals will be sacrificed 2 hours post-injection.
simt_parameters.meter: Brain tissue (cortex and hippocampus) will be collected, perfused, and sectioned. Fluorescent signal intensity of FITC-dextran and Alexa Fluor 555-BDNF will be quantified in brain parenchyma using confocal microscopy (Zeiss LSM 880) with image analysis software (ImageJ), comparing signal intensity in perivascular regions vs. deeper parenchyma. Co-localization with endothelial markers (CD31) and pericyte markers (Pdgfrb) will be assessed. Western blot analysis of tight junction proteins (Claudin-5, Occludin, ZO-1) and adherens junction proteins (VE-cadherin, N-cadherin) from isolated cerebral microvessels will be performed to assess molecular changes in BBB integrity. ELISA will quantify BDNF levels in brain homogenates.
simt_parameters.threshold_time: A significant increase (≥2-fold) in Alexa Fluor 555-BDNF brain parenchymal signal in N-cadherin activated mice compared to controls, with no significant increase (<20%) in FITC-dextran signal, within 7 days of CNO administration. This selective increase in BDNF will be correlated with increased N-cadherin expression and/or altered localization of tight junction proteins in isolated microvessels. This experiment will be completed within 3 months.

l6_tasks.expected_impact: This experiment will directly confirm if pharmacogenetic N-cadherin activation in pericytes can selectively enhance BBB permeability to NTFs without compromising inert molecule exclusion, supporting IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_01. If selective permeability is achieved, it strengthens the hypothesis that pericyte-endothelial adhesion is a key regulator of NTF transport and a viable therapeutic target. If not, it suggests N-cadherin alone is insufficient or that other mechanisms dominate.

l6_tasks.title: Time-Course Analysis of N-cadherin Activation on BBB Permeability and Pericyte-Endothelial Junction Dynamics

simt_parameters.system: Aged C57BL/6J mice (Jackson Labs, 18-20 months old, n=12 per group) expressing pericyte-specific Pdgfrb-CreERT2 and floxed DREADD for inducible N-cadherin overexpression. Aged wild-type mice will serve as controls. Mice will be housed under standard conditions.
simt_parameters.intervention: N-cadherin overexpression induced via tamoxifen (75 mg/kg, i.p., daily for 5 days). After 2 weeks washout, CNO (1 mg/kg, i.p.) will be administered daily for 1, 3, 7, and 14 days. At each time point, mice will receive intravenous injections of 10 kDa FITC-dextran and 15 kDa Alexa Fluor 555-BDNF. Control groups will receive vehicle or CNO in Cre-negative littermates at corresponding time points.
simt_parameters.meter: At each time point, brain tissue (cortex and hippocampus) will be analyzed for parenchymal fluorescence of both tracers via confocal microscopy. Additionally, electron microscopy (TEM) will be used to visualize pericyte-endothelial cell junctions and tight junctions in selected samples (Day 0, Day 7, Day 14 CNO-treated vs. controls) to assess structural changes. Immunofluorescence for N-cadherin, VE-cadherin, Claudin-5, and ZO-1 will be performed on brain sections, with quantitative analysis of junctional protein localization and intensity using ImageJ. Quantitative PCR will measure mRNA levels of key junctional proteins and N-cadherin in isolated cerebral microvessels.
simt_parameters.threshold_time: Observation of a time-dependent increase in Alexa Fluor 555-BDNF brain parenchymal signal, peaking between 3-7 days post-CNO, without a significant increase in FITC-dextran signal. TEM analysis should reveal structural changes in pericyte-endothelial contacts and/or tight junctions that correlate with the permeability changes. Significant changes (p<0.05) in junctional protein localization or expression should be detected by immunofluorescence and qPCR. This experiment will be completed within 4 months.

l6_tasks.expected_impact: This time-course study will provide critical kinetic data on how N-cadherin activation modulates BBB permeability and its impact on junctional integrity. It will help determine the optimal duration of intervention for selective NTF delivery and provide mechanistic insights into pericyte-endothelial interactions, further supporting or refuting IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_01 by detailing the dynamic response.

l6_tasks.title: Impact of Pericyte N-cadherin Activation on Hippocampal LTP and Synaptic Markers in Aged Mice

simt_parameters.system: Aged C57BL/6J mice (Jackson Labs, 18-20 months old, n=12 per group) expressing pericyte-specific Pdgfrb-CreERT2 and floxed DREADD for inducible N-cadherin overexpression. Aged wild-type mice and Cre-negative littermates will serve as controls. Mice will be housed under standard conditions.
simt_parameters.intervention: N-cadherin overexpression induced via tamoxifen (75 mg/kg, i.p., daily for 5 days). After 2 weeks washout, CNO (1 mg/kg, i.p., daily for 14 days) will be administered. During the last 7 days of CNO treatment, mice will receive daily i.p. injections of recombinant human BDNF (rhBDNF, 50 µg/kg) or vehicle (saline). Control groups will receive vehicle or CNO in Cre-negative littermates, with or without rhBDNF.
simt_parameters.meter: Ex vivo electrophysiology will be performed on hippocampal slices (CA1 region) to measure basal synaptic transmission and long-term potentiation (LTP) following theta burst stimulation. Field excitatory postsynaptic potentials (fEPSPs) will be recorded. Brain tissue will be analyzed for synaptic protein markers (e.g., PSD-95, Synaptophysin, Arc, Homer1) via Western blot and immunofluorescence in the hippocampus. ELISA will quantify BDNF levels in hippocampal homogenates. BBB permeability to 15 kDa Alexa Fluor 555-BDNF will be re-assessed at the end of the intervention period to confirm sustained permeability enhancement.
simt_parameters.threshold_time: A significant increase (≥30%) in the magnitude of LTP in N-cadherin activated mice receiving rhBDNF compared to controls, reaching at least 70% of young adult baseline LTP, within 14 days of CNO administration. This should correlate with increased synaptic protein expression (≥20%) and confirmed enhanced BDNF delivery to the hippocampus. This experiment will be completed within 4 months.

l6_tasks.expected_impact: This experiment directly tests the functional consequence of enhanced NTF delivery on synaptic plasticity, a key component of cognitive function. If LTP is improved, it strongly supports IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_01 by demonstrating that restoring pericyte-endothelial adhesion can lead to tangible neurological benefits, and that NTF delivery is a critical limiting factor in aged brains. If not, it suggests other factors limit LTP even with increased NTF availability.

l6_tasks.title: Multi-factorial Assessment of Cognitive Function and Synaptic Plasticity after Pericyte N-cadherin Activation and NTF Delivery

simt_parameters.system: Aged C57BL/6J mice (Jackson Labs, 18-20 months old, n=15 per group) expressing pericyte-specific Pdgfrb-CreERT2 and floxed DREADD for inducible N-cadherin overexpression. Aged wild-type and Cre-negative littermates will serve as controls. Mice will be housed under standard conditions.
simt_parameters.intervention: N-cadherin overexpression induced via tamoxifen (75 mg/kg, i.p., daily for 5 days). After 2 weeks washout, mice will be divided into 4 intervention groups for 4 weeks: 1) Vehicle + Saline, 2) CNO (1 mg/kg, i.p., daily) + Saline, 3) Vehicle + rhBDNF (50 µg/kg, i.p., daily), 4) CNO + rhBDNF. Cognitive testing will commence after 2 weeks of CNO/rhBDNF administration and continue for 2 weeks. A separate cohort will receive 10 kDa FITC-dextran and 15 kDa Alexa Fluor 555-BDNF at the end of the 4-week intervention for BBB permeability assessment.
simt_parameters.meter: Cognitive function will be assessed using a battery of behavioral tests: Morris Water Maze (spatial learning and memory), Novel Object Recognition (recognition memory), and Fear Conditioning (associative memory). Electrophysiological recordings of LTP will be performed in hippocampal slices from a subset of animals. Synaptic density (using unbiased stereology and immunofluorescence for PSD-95 and Synaptophysin) and dendritic spine morphology (Golgi staining) will be quantified in hippocampal CA1 and dentate gyrus. RNA sequencing (Illumina NovaSeq) of hippocampal tissue will be performed to identify changes in gene expression related to synaptic plasticity, neuroinflammation, and neurogenesis.
simt_parameters.threshold_time: A significant improvement (e.g., reduced latency and increased time in target quadrant in MWM, increased discrimination index in NOR, increased freezing time in fear conditioning, all p<0.05) in cognitive performance in the CNO+rhBDNF group compared to all other groups, reaching at least 80% of young adult baseline performance. This improvement should correlate with enhanced LTP magnitude (≥30%), increased synaptic density (≥15%), and favorable gene expression changes (FDR < 0.05, |log2FC| > 1) in synaptic plasticity pathways. This experiment will be completed within 6 months.

l6_tasks.expected_impact: This comprehensive, multi-factorial experiment will provide robust evidence for the therapeutic efficacy of pericyte N-cadherin activation combined with systemic NTF delivery. By integrating behavioral, electrophysiological, morphological, and transcriptomic data, it will provide a holistic view of the intervention's impact on brain function, strongly supporting or refuting IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_01 and guiding future therapeutic development.

l6_tasks.title: Assessment of Off-Target Effects of Pericyte N-cadherin Activation on Cerebrovascular and Systemic Health

simt_parameters.system: Aged C57BL/6J mice (Jackson Labs, 18-20 months old, n=10 per group) expressing pericyte-specific Pdgfrb-CreERT2 and floxed DREADD for inducible N-cadherin overexpression. Aged wild-type and Cre-negative littermates will serve as controls. Mice will be housed under standard conditions.
simt_parameters.intervention: N-cadherin overexpression induced via tamoxifen (75 mg/kg, i.p., daily for 5 days). After 2 weeks washout, CNO (1 mg/kg, i.p., daily for 14 days) will be administered. Control groups will receive vehicle or CNO in Cre-negative littermates.
simt_parameters.meter: Immunofluorescence will be performed on brain sections to assess activation/reactivity of astrocytes (GFAP), microglia (Iba1), and endothelial cells (CD31, VE-cadherin expression/localization). Western blot will quantify N-cadherin and VE-cadherin expression in isolated endothelial cells and astrocytes (if feasible to isolate). Systemic blood samples will be collected for complete blood count (CBC), liver and kidney function tests (ALT, AST, creatinine, BUN), and inflammatory cytokine panel (Luminex multiplex assay, e.g., IL-6, TNF-α). Blood pressure will be monitored via tail-cuff method weekly. Brain MRI will be performed to detect any gross structural changes or microhemorrhages.
simt_parameters.threshold_time: No significant changes (p>0.05) in GFAP or Iba1 reactivity, endothelial cell junctional protein expression/localization, systemic inflammatory markers, blood pressure, or liver/kidney function in N-cadherin activated mice compared to controls. MRI should show no evidence of microhemorrhages or other structural abnormalities. This experiment will be completed within 3 months.

l6_tasks.expected_impact: This experiment will provide crucial safety data and confirm the specificity of the pericyte-targeted intervention. If no significant off-target effects are observed, it strengthens the validity of attributing observed functional improvements to pericyte-endothelial adhesion modulation, further supporting IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_01. If off-target effects are detected, it necessitates re-evaluation of the intervention strategy and potential confounding factors.

l6_tasks.title: Transcriptomic Analysis of Pericyte-Specific N-cadherin Activation on Cerebrovascular Cell Types

simt_parameters.system: Aged C57BL/6J mice (Jackson Labs, 18-20 months old, n=8 per group) expressing pericyte-specific Pdgfrb-CreERT2 and floxed DREADD for inducible N-cadherin overexpression. Aged wild-type and Cre-negative littermates will serve as controls. Mice will be housed under standard conditions.
simt_parameters.intervention: N-cadherin overexpression induced via tamoxifen (75 mg/kg, i.p., daily for 5 days). After 2 weeks washout, CNO (1 mg/kg, i.p., daily for 7 days) will be administered. Control groups will receive vehicle or CNO in Cre-negative littermates.
simt_parameters.meter: Single-nucleus RNA sequencing (snRNA-seq) will be performed on isolated nuclei from cortical and hippocampal tissue. Cell type-specific transcriptomic changes will be analyzed for pericytes, endothelial cells, astrocytes, and microglia. Differential gene expression analysis (FDR < 0.05, |log2FC| > 0.5) will identify changes in pathways related to cell adhesion, junctional integrity, inflammatory response, and metabolic function in each cell type. Specific attention will be paid to changes in endothelial cells and astrocytes to detect unintended responses.
simt_parameters.threshold_time: Significant transcriptomic changes (FDR < 0.05) should be primarily observed in pericytes, with minimal or no significant changes (e.g., less than 5% of differentially expressed genes) in endothelial cells, astrocytes, or microglia, particularly in genes related to barrier function or inflammatory activation. This experiment will be completed within 4 months.

l6_tasks.expected_impact: This unbiased transcriptomic approach will provide a high-resolution view of the cellular responses to pericyte N-cadherin activation, confirming the cell-type specificity of the intervention. If changes are largely confined to pericytes, it reinforces the targeted nature of the intervention and supports IH_Q_L3_M_G1_1_01. If significant off-target transcriptional changes are observed in endothelial cells or other cell types, it suggests broader, potentially confounding effects that need to be considered.
